content.text: Why Release Freezes Are a Budget Policy, Not a Calendar Event

Traditional release freezes are time-based: no deployments during Q4 holidays, during a major sporting event, or before a board presentation. These freezes protect against the risk of a bad deploy during high-traffic periods. Error budget freezes are different in nature. They are triggered not by calendar dates but by the objective signal that the system is already degraded relative to its committed SLO — meaning the window for safe experimentation has shrunk or closed entirely.

The policy logic is simple: error budget represents the permitted amount of unreliability. When it is nearly exhausted, every additional deployment adds risk to a system that is already at the edge of its reliability commitment. A new feature deploy that adds even a small amount of additional failure is qualitatively different when the budget is at 3% remaining versus 80% remaining. The freeze is not punitive — it is a feedback mechanism that connects engineering velocity to the real-world reliability signal.

attrs.alt: Release freeze tiers: Normal, Soft Freeze, Hard Freeze, and Budget Exhausted zones with lifecycle flow

content.text: Most teams implement three freeze tiers, each triggered by a different budget threshold. The thresholds below are common starting points; calibrate to your own release cadence and SLO windows.
New feature deploys; scope-expanding config changes
All application deploys; prompt / model changes

Threshold calibration: the 20% and 5% values come from a 30-day budget window. For a 7-day eval quality SLO, these fractions correspond to much shorter absolute times — 1.4 days and 8.4 hours respectively. Consider adjusting thresholds for shorter-window SLOs so that the freeze gives teams enough time to respond.

A freeze policy that relies on engineers voluntarily checking a dashboard before deploying will fail under pressure. The enforcement must be automated — a step in the deployment pipeline that queries current budget status and blocks the deploy if the freeze level prohibits it. The gate runs on every deploy, including rollbacks, which are then explicitly allow-listed based on deploy type.

The gate distinguishes between deploy types explicitly. A 'rollback' is almost always safe to allow under a soft or hard freeze because it reduces scope, not increases it. A 'hotfix' is allowed under soft freeze because it targets a specific known-bad behavior. A 'feature' deploy is the highest-risk category and is what the freeze primarily targets.
Wire the gate into your CI/CD pipeline so that it runs automatically on every push to main, with a manual override path for approved exceptions.

# .github/workflows/deploy-gate.yml
name: Deploy Gate

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      deploy_type:
        description: 'Deploy type (feature/hotfix/rollback)'
        required: true
        default: 'feature'
      override_approved:
        description: 'Freeze override approved (true/false)'
        required: false
        default: 'false'

jobs:
  budget-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check error budget freeze status
        env:
          PROMETHEUS_URL: ${{ secrets.PROMETHEUS_URL }}
        run: |
          DEPLOY_TYPE=${{ github.event.inputs.deploy_type || 'feature' }}
          OVERRIDE=${{ github.event.inputs.override_approved || 'false' }}
          python scripts/freeze_gate.py $DEPLOY_TYPE $OVERRIDE

      - name: Deploy application
        if: success()
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh

The workflow_dispatch trigger allows manual invocations with override_approved=true for cases where the SRE on-call or Engineering VP has approved an exception. The approval decision is captured in the workflow run parameters, providing an audit trail in GitHub Actions history.

Engineers attempting a blocked deploy need immediate, clear communication: what the current freeze level is, why it is active (which SLO's budget triggered it), and what the exception path looks like. Slack notifications on state transitions also catch teams before they even attempt a deploy.

# freeze_notify.py  — posts freeze state changes to Slack
import requests, os
from freeze_gate import BudgetStatus, FreezeLevel

SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ['SLACK_DEPLOY_WEBHOOK']

LEVEL_EMOJI = {
    FreezeLevel.NORMAL:    ':white_check_mark:',
    FreezeLevel.SOFT:      ':warning:',
    FreezeLevel.HARD:      ':no_entry:',
    FreezeLevel.EXHAUSTED: ':rotating_light:',
}

def notify_freeze_change(
    prev_level: FreezeLevel,
    new_level: FreezeLevel,
    statuses: list[BudgetStatus],
) -> None:
    if prev_level == new_level:
        return
    emoji = LEVEL_EMOJI[new_level]
    budget_lines = [
        f'  • {s.slo_id}: {s.budget_remaining_fraction:.1%} remaining'
        for s in statuses
    ]
    message = (
        f'{emoji} *Deploy freeze state changed*: '
        f'{prev_level.value} → {new_level.value}\n'
        + '\n'.join(budget_lines)
    )
    if new_level in (FreezeLevel.HARD, FreezeLevel.EXHAUSTED):
        message += (
            '\n\nAll deploys blocked. '
            'See runbook: https://wiki/runbooks/release-freeze'
        )
    requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={'text': message}, timeout=5)

Every freeze policy needs an exception process, otherwise teams will route around it by labeling everything a 'hotfix.' The exception process must create accountability — the approver is on record — while being fast enough that a true emergency does not stall. A synchronous Slack thread with the on-call SRE or Engineering VP, producing a named decision, takes 5–10 minutes and satisfies both requirements.
Why deploy cannot wait; risk assessment for the specific change
Business necessity; what mitigation is in place if the deploy degrades the SLO further

For GenAI systems specifically, 'prompt changes' and 'model version changes' should be treated as equivalent to feature deploys in the exception policy. A prompt change can shift the distribution of model outputs in ways that affect eval quality, safety, and latency simultaneously. Do not give prompt changes a softer exception path than code changes.

Each freeze activation should produce a tracking ticket. This serves two purposes: it creates a permanent record of when the freeze was active and what decisions were made during it, and it gives stakeholders a single place to monitor the situation rather than polling the SRE on-call repeatedly.

# jira_freeze_ticket.py  — creates a Jira tracking ticket when freeze activates
import os
from jira import JIRA
from freeze_gate import BudgetStatus, FreezeLevel

def create_freeze_ticket(
    jira_client: JIRA,
    project_key: str,
    freeze_level: FreezeLevel,
    statuses: list[BudgetStatus],
) -> str:
    priority = 'Critical' if freeze_level == FreezeLevel.HARD else 'High'
    budget_summary = ', '.join(
        f'{s.slo_id}={s.budget_remaining_fraction:.1%}'
        for s in statuses
    )
    issue = jira_client.create_issue(
        project=project_key,
        summary=f'[FREEZE-{freeze_level.value.upper()}] Error budget below threshold',
        issuetype={'name': 'Incident'},
        priority={'name': priority},
        description=(
            f'Error budget freeze activated: {freeze_level.value}\n\n'
            f'Budget remaining: {budget_summary}\n\n'
            'All deployments blocked per freeze policy.\n'
            'See runbook: https://wiki/runbooks/release-freeze\n\n'
            'Resolution: budget must recover above threshold for 24h to lift freeze.'
        ),
    )
    return issue.key

A freeze lifts when the budget has recovered above the threshold — but not just at a single instant. Burn rate can oscillate. If the freeze lifts the moment budget crosses the threshold and then the budget immediately falls back below it, teams will experience confusing 'flapping' freeze states. The standard mitigation is a 24-hour stability requirement: the budget must remain above the threshold continuously for 24 hours before the freeze is lifted.

# lift_freeze_check.py — checks if freeze can be lifted
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from freeze_gate import fetch_budget_status, FreezeLevel
import os

def can_lift_freeze(
    current_statuses: list,
    historical_statuses: list,  # last 24h readings
    freeze_level: FreezeLevel,
) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    A freeze lifts when budget has recovered above the threshold
    for at least 24 consecutive hours — not just at one instant.
    This prevents oscillation caused by the burn rate crossing
    the threshold briefly and triggering a flapping cycle.
    """
    if freeze_level == FreezeLevel.NORMAL:
        return True, 'Already in normal state'

    threshold = 0.20 if freeze_level == FreezeLevel.SOFT else 0.05

    # All readings in last 24h must be above threshold
    all_above = all(
        s.budget_remaining_fraction > threshold
        for s in historical_statuses
    )

    if all_above and len(historical_statuses) >= 24:
        return True, (
            f'Budget above {threshold:.0%} for 24h — freeze can be lifted'
        )
    return False, (
        f'Budget not yet stable above {threshold:.0%} for 24h '
        f'({len(historical_statuses)} readings checked)'
    )
Do not lift a hard freeze just because the burn rate decreased — the budget must actually recover above the threshold.
When lifting, post to the same Slack channel that announced the freeze.
Update the Jira ticket with root cause summary before closing.
Perform a brief retro on what caused the budget drain before resuming normal velocity.
GenAI systems have several properties that complicate standard release freeze policies.
Budget recovery takes days, not hours, making the freeze potentially long
Implement per-SLO freeze levels; eval freeze may lift sooner if the eval pass rate recovers even if the 7-day average is still low
Model provider incidents burn availability budget
Budget exhaustion may not be caused by engineering changes at all
Track provider-caused budget burns separately; consider a freeze waiver for incidents caused entirely by upstream providers
Prompt changes are invisible to standard deploy gates
A prompt pushed via a config management system bypasses the CI/CD gate
Apply freeze checks to config management pipeline; treat prompt changes as deploys in the gate logic
Safety SLO burn requires immediate action regardless of freeze level
A safety freeze exception process that takes even 10 minutes is too slow
For safety SLO exhaustion only, disable feature flags automatically first and investigate second — do not wait for approval
Setting thresholds too low: triggering the soft freeze at 5% gives teams almost no time to respond before the hard freeze fires. 20% and 5% give two meaningful tiers with distinct response times.
Not tracking freeze duration: teams that do not measure how long they spend in soft or hard freeze have no signal to improve their burn rate management.

Exception approval by the same on-call who is trying to deploy: the approver must be someone not directly involved in the deploy decision. An SRE approving their own team's feature deploy under soft freeze is not a real check.

Forgetting configuration and data pipeline changes: database migrations, model weight updates, and A/B test configuration changes can burn budget just as fast as code deploys. The freeze policy must cover all change types.

Release freeze policies tie engineering velocity directly to reliability signals. Three tiers — soft freeze, hard freeze, and budget-exhausted — each with distinct allowed change types and approval chains, give teams a proportionate response to deteriorating budget health. Automated CI/CD gate enforcement removes the reliance on manual discipline, while the exception process ensures that genuine emergencies can still be addressed with accountability. The 24-hour stability requirement for lifting a freeze prevents flapping. For GenAI systems, extend the policy to cover prompt changes, model version changes, and provider-incident budget burns with appropriate handling for each.
